title: Mosaic: An Extensible Framework for Composing Rule-Based and Learned Motion Planners
abstract: 安全且可解释的路径规划仍是自动驾驶领域的核心挑战。基于规则的规划器虽能提供可预测且可解释的行为，但往往难以应对真实交通场景的复杂性和不确定性。而基于学习的规划器虽具备强大适应性，却存在透明度降低和偶发安全违规问题。我们提出Mosaic框架——一个通过仲裁图融合两种范式的可扩展结构化决策系统。通过将轨迹验证与评分从各规划器独立生成的轨迹中解耦，每个决策都变得透明可追溯。高层级的轨迹验证引入了规划器间的冗余机制，将紧急制动限制在极少数所有规划器均无法生成有效轨迹的场景中。通过统一评分和最优轨迹选择，具有互补优势的基于规则与基于学习的规划器得以融合，实现两者的最优协同。在nuPlan实验评估中，Mosaic在Val14闭环基准测试中分别达到95.48 CLS-NR和93.98 CLS-R，创下新纪录，同时将责任碰撞率相比各子规划器单独运行降低30%。在聚焦高度交互性困难场景的interPlan基准测试中，Mosaic获得54.30 CLS-R评分，较最优子规划器提升23.3%——全程无需重新训练或额外数据支撑。代码已开源至github.com/KIT-MRT/mosaic。
html_content:
\svgpath
figures/
Mosaic: An Extensible Framework for Composing
Rule-Based and Learned Motion Planners
Nick Le Large
*
1
,
Marlon Steiner
*
1
,
Lingguang Wang
1
,
Willi Poh
1
,
Jan-Hendrik Pauls
1
,
Ömer Şahin Taş
2
and
Christoph Stiller
1
* Authors have equal contribution.
1
Institute of Measurement and Control Systems, Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT),
Karlsruhe, Germany
{firstname.lastname}@kit.edu
2
FZI Research Center for Information Technology, Karlsruhe, Germany
{lastname}@fzi.de
Abstract
Safe and explainable motion planning remains a central challenge in autonomous driving.
While rule-based planners offer predictable and explainable behavior, they often fail to grasp the complexity and uncertainty of real-world traffic.
Conversely, learned planners exhibit strong adaptability but suffer from reduced transparency and occasional safety violations.
We introduce
Mosaic
, an extensible framework for structured decision-making that integrates both paradigms through arbitration graphs.
By decoupling trajectory verification and scoring from the generation of trajectories by individual planners, every decision becomes transparent and traceable.
Trajectory verification at a higher level introduces redundancy between the planners, limiting emergency braking to the rare case where all planners fail to produce a valid trajectory.
Through unified scoring and optimal trajectory selection, rule-based and learned planners with complementary strengths and weaknesses can be combined to yield the best of both worlds.
In experimental evaluation on nuPlan,
Mosaic
achieves
95.48
\mathbf{95.48}
CLS-NR and
93.98
\mathbf{93.98}
CLS-R
on the Val14 closed-loop benchmark, setting a new state of the art, while reducing at-fault collisions by 30% compared to either planner in isolation.
On the interPlan benchmark, focused on highly interactive and difficult scenarios,
Mosaic
scores
54.30
\mathbf{54.30}
CLS-R
, outperforming its best constituent planner by 23.3% — all without retraining or requiring additional data.
The code is available at
github.com/KIT-MRT/mosaic
.
I
Introduction
Motion planning is a fundamental task for autonomous driving, responsible for generating safe, comfortable, and goal-directed trajectories in dynamic traffic environments.
Achieving these objectives simultaneously remains a significant challenge, as planners must adapt to diverse scenarios and remain explainable to ensure trust and verifiability.
Existing motion planning approaches often fall into three main categories. Rule-based planners
[
undef
,
undefa
]
encode human-designed heuristics and constraints to produce predictable and explainable behaviors, but they can struggle to adapt to complex, unstructured, or previously unseen situations. Learning-based planners
[
undefa
,
undefb
,
undefc
,
undefd
,
undefe
]
, on the other hand, demonstrate strong adaptability by leveraging data-driven models to capture the rich variability of real-world driving.
However, their decision-making processes are not directly accessible, making it difficult to interpret or verify their outputs—a limitation that impedes their deployment in safety-critical systems.
Lastly, hybrid approaches
[
undefa
,
undeff
,
undefb
]
often combine learned planners with rules as a post-processing step. These approaches are mostly non-unified and tailored to the individual needs of a specific use case.
Figure 1
:
Mosaic
combines complementary planners—e. g. rule-based and learned—within a structured and extensible framework. An arbitrator selects the best trajectory (
green
) from candidate proposals (
blue
) while a shared verification layer rejects unsafe plans (
red
), enabling safe and explainable motion planning.
In this work, we propose a unified framework for motion planning based on arbitration graphs (AGs)
[
undefg
,
undefh
]
, which are a structured decision-making mechanism that enables coordination among multiple behavior components.
We call our framework
Mosaic
(see
Fig.
˜
1
), as it combines heterogeneous planning approaches—much like the art form that assembles diverse pieces into a beautiful, coherent whole that is greater than the sum of its parts.
While many state-of-the-art motion planners include some kind of post-processing or internal verification, we propose to structurally separate trajectory generation, the strengths of individual planning approaches, from trajectory verification and scoring.
Building upon the arbitration concept, a cost arbitrator can evaluate the trajectory proposals of two or more complementary planners, e. g. a rule-based and a learned approach.
Within our work, we wrap the learning-based planner FlowDrive*
[
undefd
]
and the rule-based planner PDM-Closed
[
undefa
]
into the AG.
Through unified verification, unsafe proposals that would lead to collisions can be rejected, while a fallback behavior—emergency braking—integrates naturally as a safety layer within the arbitration graph.
A unified scoring function, inspired by the nuPlan metric, allows choosing the best proposal and thus combining the strengths of both approaches.
Finally, the concept is extensible to additional motion planners and fallback layers.
At the same time, it makes it clear which trajectory is chosen and why, i. e. due to cost or validity, enabling not only safe and optimal but also explainable decision-making for autonomous driving.
Our contributions are as follows:
•
We introduce
Mosaic
, an extensible and explainable motion planning framework based on arbitration graphs that can integrate any planning algorithm—regardless of whether it is rule-based, learned, or hybrid—into a structured and extensible decision-making structure.
•
We decouple trajectory verification and scoring from the trajectory proposal, the strengths of individual planners, and embed them within the arbitration mechanism, enabling transparent, traceable, and adjustable decision-making at the behavior selection level.
By rejecting invalid trajectories, we can reduce collisions by 30% compared to either planner in isolation.
A unified scoring function, inspired by the nuPlan metric, allows for choosing the best trajectory proposal for any given situation, utilizing the complementary strengths of both planning approaches.
This leads to a reduction in zero-score trajectories by 33% and outperforms or matches both individual planners in 11 of 14 scenario types.
•
In our experimental evaluation, we achieve state-of-the-art performance on both the nuPlan closed-loop non-reactive and reactive benchmarks (Val14 split
[
undefa
]
), outperforming all existing methods—rule-based, learned, and hybrid approaches—without retraining the underlying planners or requiring additional data.
II
Related Work
Autonomous driving requires reliable motion planning capable of handling diverse environments, uncertain interactions, and safety-critical decisions.
This section reviews prior work on motion planning paradigms and on decision-making frameworks based on AGs, highlighting the need for a unified and interpretable integration mechanism across potentially heterogeneous motion planners.
II-A
Motion Planning
Motion planning for autonomous driving can be broadly categorized into
rule-based
,
learning-based
, and
hybrid
approaches, with recent trends extending toward generative and language-based models.
Rule-based methods
rely on deterministic algorithms
[
undef
]
, handcrafted rules
[
undefa
]
, or optimization-based formulations
[
undefi
]
to generate feasible and interpretable trajectories.
While such methods offer transparency and predictability, they typically struggle in highly dynamic or unstructured environments.
Learning-based approaches
[
undefj
,
undefb
,
undefk
,
undefl
]
leverage neural architectures, often transformers, to learn motion planning directly from data.
These models exhibit adaptability and generalization across complex scenarios.
In addition to imitation learning, reinforcement learning (RL)
[
undefm
,
undefe
]
, where agents learn driving policies through interaction and reward feedback, has been widely explored for motion planning.
RL-based planners have shown promise in closed-loop control
[
undefe
]
and adaptation to rare scenarios, but often require extensive training and safety constraints.
End-to-end approaches
[
undefn
,
undefo
,
undefp
,
undefq
]
further integrate perception into the planning task, directly mapping sensor inputs to control commands or trajectories.
Recently, generative models such as diffusion- or flow-based planners
[
undefd
,
undefr
,
undefs
,
undefc
,
undeft
]
have emerged as powerful tools for producing diverse, realistic, and multimodal trajectory distributions.
In parallel, language-driven methods explore the integration of natural language reasoning into driving systems, leveraging large language models (LLMs)
[
undefu
,
undefv
]
and vision language models (VLMs)
[
undefw
,
undefx
]
to explain semantic instructions and contextual cues.
Despite their variety, most learning-based planning paradigms rely on
hybrid methods
[
undeff
,
undeft
,
undefa
]
with a fixed internal decision process as post-processing to choose or refine the final trajectory.
Note that there are also approaches utilizing learned methods to refine the rule-based trajectory
[
undefa
,
undefy
]
.
When designing such a hybrid method, the decision logic is often ad hoc, non-modular, or hard to reproduce.
This lack of a unified arbitration mechanism limits interpretability, extensibility, and safe integration of diverse planning modules.
II-B
Decision-Making and Arbitration Graphs
Figure 2
:
The proposed AG structure with 3 behavior components (
FlowDrive*
,
PDM-Closed
,
Emergency Stop
), a cost arbitrator
Composer
, and a priority arbitrator
Mosaic
.
AGs provide a structured and explainable mechanism for hierarchical decision-making in autonomous systems
[
undefh
]
.
Originally developed in the context of robotic soccer
[
undefg
]
and later used in autonomous driving
[
undefz
]
, AGs draw inspiration from Brooks’ behavior-based subsumption, knowledge-based architectures like Belief-Desire-Intention, and object-oriented design principles
[
undefh
]
.
In this framework, atomic
behavior components
represent fundamental abilities or behaviors that interpret the current situation and propose
commands
when their
invocation conditions
are met.
Arbitrators organize these behaviors hierarchically, filtering applicable options and selecting the most suitable one for execution using strategies such as priority- or cost-based selection.
Compared to Behavior Trees, AGs explicitly encode preconditions and decision logic, leading to greater robustness and transparency.
Recent developments extend AGs with domain-specific
verification mechanisms
that validate the safety and feasibility of proposed commands before execution
[
undefh
]
.
This enables the integration of experimental or learned behaviors without compromising system reliability, as unsafe or invalid outputs are intercepted in a verification step and mitigated through structured fallback layers.
However, prior work has not explored the use of arbitration graphs on real-world data to evaluate the effectiveness of this approach on a widely recognized benchmark.
In contrast, our work applies AGs to the large-scale autonomous driving benchmark nuPlan
[
undefaa
]
and demonstrates that they can serve as an explainable decision structure capable of integrating heterogeneous planners within a single framework.
Notably, AGs can serve not only as a method to combine multiple planners, but also to wrap a single planner, e.g., a learning-based one, into an explainable framework.
Therefore, AGs are also suitable for replacing opaque, non-unified and specifically tailored decision-making, which is built on top of most of the current learning-based planning approaches
[
undefa
,
undefb
,
undeft
,
undeff
]
.
III
The Mosaic Framework
The overall architecture of
Mosaic
is shown in
Fig.
˜
2
.
We make use of the explainable AG concept to compose two planners into a single architecture.
Briefly, the framework can be understood as follows (from left to right, see
Fig.
˜
2
):
•
FlowDrive*
/
PDM-Closed
: These components correspond to behaviors in the AG framework, each proposing a trajectory given the current state of the world (see
Section
˜
III-A
).
•
Composer
: This element is a cost arbitrator (see
Section
˜
III-C
) with an integrated verification step. For each behavior, the arbitrator verifies the proposed trajectory (see
Section
˜
III-B
); only verified trajectories are scored and considered for selection.
•
Emergency Stop
: This behavior computes an emergency braking trajectory. As indicated by the safety buoy, this is a last resort fallback which does not need to pass verification
[
undefh
]
.
•
Mosaic
: This is the root arbitrator and it is designed as a priority arbitrator (see
Section
˜
III-D
). If the
Composer
arbitrator provides a valid trajectory, this trajectory is chosen, otherwise the
Emergency Stop
trajectory is applied.
Throughout this paper, we use
monospace
font to refer to behavior components within the AG, which wrap the respective planner and include the modifications described in
Section
˜
III-B
.
Plain text refers to the underlying planner itself as presented in the original publications.
III-A
Trajectory Planners as Behaviors in the AG
As
Mosaic
is planner-agnostic, we demonstrate it with two complementary planners in the AG.
To combine the strengths of different motion planning paradigms, we choose a rule-based planner (PDM-Closed) and a learning-based planner (FlowDrive*).
While FlowDrive
[
undefd
]
is the current open-source SOTA model, PDM-Closed achieves strong results as well on the nuPlan closed-loop benchmark.
A rule-based and learning-based motion planner combination was already introduced through PDM-Hybrid (PDM-Closed + PDM-Open)
[
undefa
]
, achieving slightly worse results in the closed-loop benchmark than PDM-Closed as a stand-alone.
Contrary to this, we propose the use of a generic AG framework to fuse different planners into an explainable structure, naturally including trajectory verification and scoring, as well as fallback maneuvers.
In the following, we provide a brief description of the utilized planners.
III-A
1
Learning-based FlowDrive Planner
The FlowDrive model
[
undefd
]
utilizes rectified flow-matching
[
undefab
,
undefac
]
for trajectory generation. This model addresses the common issue of data imbalance in driving datasets.
While FlowDrive represents a purely learned approach based on a diffusion transformer architecture
[
undefad
]
, the authors also provide an extension of the model using post-processing, resulting in a hybrid planning method (FlowDrive*).
FlowDrive* features a moderated guidance mechanism that injects small perturbations during trajectory generation to increase maneuver diversity, such as overtaking or nudging.
Evaluated on the nuPlan and interPlan
[
undefae
]
benchmarks, FlowDrive* achieves SOTA results among open-source methods, surpassing previous learning-based and hybrid methods.
We utilize FlowDrive* in a behavior component of the AG presented in this work.
III-A
2
Rule-based PDM-Closed Planner
The Predictive Driver Model Closed (PDM-Closed)
[
undefa
]
is a rule-based planner that extends the established Intelligent Driver Model (IDM).
It is designed to maximize performance in the nuPlan closed-loop evaluation task.
The model incorporates concepts from model predictive control, involving forecasting, proposal generation, simulation, scoring, and trajectory selection.
It first uses a graph search to select a sequence of lanes (centerline) to follow.
To handle diverse scenarios and address the trade-offs found when selecting a single target speed, PDM-Closed generates 15 trajectory proposals. These proposals are created by implementing IDM policies across five distinct target speeds (20% to 100% of the speed limit) at three lateral centerline offsets (±1 m and 0 m). The environment forecasting uses a simple constant velocity forecast for other dynamic agents.
The trajectory proposals are simulated based on the expected closed-loop movement (using an LQR controller and a kinematic bicycle model).
They are then scored based on metrics favoring traffic-rule compliance, progress, and comfort.
An emergency brake is enforced if a collision is anticipated within two seconds.
At publication time, PDM-Closed achieved SOTA closed-loop performance on nuPlan, while its open-loop performance is very poor.
III-B
Behavior Verification
Both PDM-Closed and FlowDrive* run an internal check and fall back to emergency braking if an at-fault collision is expected within
2.0
s
2.0\text{\,}\mathrm{s}
.
In this work, we turn off these internal checks and move them into the arbitration graph.
As introduced in
[
undefh
]
, verification is integrated directly into the arbitration algorithm: before an option is selected, the arbitrator performs a domain-specific verification of its proposed command.
If the command fails the verification, it is discarded and the arbitrator proceeds to the next best option (see
Fig.
˜
3
).
This allows
Mosaic
to clearly communicate which trajectories are rejected and why, while falling back on the emergency stop maneuver only if both
PDM-Closed
and
FlowDrive*
propose unsafe options at the same time (see
Section
˜
III-D
).
By centralizing verification within the arbitration graph, unsafe or experimental motion planners can be integrated without compromising the defined safety bounds.
Figure 3
:
Behavior verification: One behavior component fails verification (
red
) and is rejected. The remaining behavior is selected.
III-C
The Cost Arbitrator
After filtering unsafe trajectories, the cost arbitrator (see
Composer
in
Fig.
˜
2
) applies a cost function to select the best remaining option.
Our scoring function is inspired by the nuPlan closed-loop evaluation metrics but, crucially, replaces their binary pass/fail decisions with
continuous
scores in
[
0
,
1
]
[0,1]
, giving the cost arbitrator fine-grained information to distinguish between candidate trajectories.
Of course, unlike the nuPlan evaluation metrics, our scores have to be computed
online
using only historical observations and predictions and cannot rely on ground truth data.
We calculate a total score
S
total
S_{\mathrm{total}}
for each trajectory using a multiplicative gating approach:
S
total
=
S
coll
⋅
S
driv
⋅
S

Safety gate.
The three multiplicative safety metrics act as a gate: if any is zero, the entire score collapses to zero, ensuring that risky trajectories are never preferred regardless of their performance.
The collision score
S
coll
S_{\mathrm{coll}}
is computed as
1
1
minus the overlap ratio between the ego polygon and the colliding object, so that a grazing contact is penalized less than a full overlap.
The drivable area score
S
driv
S_{\mathrm{driv}}
returns the fraction of the simulated trajectory that remains within the drivable area.
The driving direction score
S
dir
S_{\mathrm{dir}}
penalizes oncoming-traffic travel based on the cumulative distance driven against traffic.
Progress gate.
A soft gate
G

G_{\mathrm{prog}}=\min(\text{progress}/\tau,\;1)
penalizes standstill trajectories, where progress is the ego’s displacement along the route centerline normalized by the expected distance given the current speed.
Performance score.
The performance score
S
perf
S_{\mathrm{perf}}
is a weighted average of progress, time-to-collision (TTC), and comfort, with weights listed in

I

The TTC score is the predicted time to the earliest collision, clamped to a
3

horizon and normalized to

, yielding a continuous penalty that increases as a collision approaches.
The comfort score evaluates acceleration and jerk constraints along the trajectory.

III-D
Priority Arbitrator to Integrate Fallback Layer
Figure 4
:
Emergency fallback: Both behavior components fail verification. The
Composer
becomes inapplicable, and the priority arbitrator falls back to
Emergency Stop
.
As the last step in our AG design, we use a priority arbitrator (
Mosaic
in
Fig.
˜
2
), which selects valid options top-down.
A trajectory from the cost arbitrator is always preferred over the
Emergency Stop
behavior.
If neither
FlowDrive*
nor
PDM-Closed
passes verification, the
Composer
arbitrator becomes inapplicable and the priority arbitrator falls back to the
Emergency Stop
behavior (see
Fig.
˜
4
).
Following
[
undefh
]
, the emergency stop serves as the last resort fallback and does not need to pass verification.
It is implemented as a straight-line deceleration, ensuring a simple and deterministic action.
Compared to the emergency stop planner utilized both in FlowDrive* and PDM-Closed, we increased the deceleration from
4.05
m
s
−
2
4.05\text{\,}\mathrm{m}\text{\,}{\mathrm{s}}^{-2}
to
8.0
m
s
−
2
8.0\text{\,}\mathrm{m}\text{\,}{\mathrm{s}}^{-2}
.
IV
Evaluation on nuPlan
We evaluate our proposed
Mosaic
framework on the nuPlan closed-loop benchmark and the interPlan benchmark, comparing against a wide range of motion planners, including the current learned SOTA method GIGAFLOW
[
undefe
]
.
IV-A
nuPlan Closed-loop Benchmark
Following the evaluation established by
[
undefa
]
, we evaluate the Val14 split of the nuPlan benchmark, which comprises
1118
1118
scenarios across 14 scenario types.
The closed-loop benchmark computes two scores (CLS): CLS-NR (non-reactive) and CLS-R (reactive), which refer to the movement of other agents.
The final score accounts for at-fault collisions, drivable area compliance, driving direction compliance, progress made, time to collision, speed limit compliance, ego progress along the expert’s route ratio, and comfort.
We additionally evaluate on the interPlan benchmark
[
undefae
]
, a publicly available nuPlan extension focused on highly interactive and difficult scenarios.
IV-B
Baselines
We compare against a comprehensive set of learned, rule-based, and hybrid planning methods.
In the category of purely learned planners, the current open-source SOTA is FlowDrive
[
undefd
]
, which utilizes the generative flow matching principle and is trained with a scenario sampling strategy that leads to more balanced data.
The closed-source
GIGAFLOW
[
undefe
]
model is also a learned planner, trained purely in self-play simulation within a multi-agent reinforcement learning setting without seeing any data from the dataset.
In the category of hybrid methods,
FlowDrive*
with moderated guidance and a trajectory selection process leads the benchmark of open-source models.
IV-C
Quantitative Results
Table
II
summarizes the results on the Val14 benchmark. Among purely learning-based planners, diffusion-based
[
undeft
]
and flow-matching
[
undefd
]
models achieve the highest closed-loop scores, surpassing earlier methods
[
undefj
,
undefb
]
. However, a consistent trend can be observed across all entries: when a rule-based component is integrated, forming a hybrid system, the resulting performance increases substantially
[
undefb
,
undeft
,
undefd
]
.
This highlights the crucial role of rule-based reasoning in enhancing the stability and safety of data-driven planners. Consequently, hybrid planning strategies currently represent the most effective class of motion planners on nuPlan.
Our proposed
Mosaic
framework achieves
95.48
95.48
CLS-NR and
93.98
93.98
CLS-R, surpassing all baselines and setting a new SOTA on the nuPlan closed-loop non-reactive as well as the closed-loop reactive benchmarks.
On the interPlan benchmark,
Mosaic
achieves
54.30
54.30
CLS-R
, outperforming its components FlowDrive* by
23.3
%
23.3\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
resp. PDM-Closed by
31.7
%
31.7\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
.
These results demonstrate that the proposed AG framework is able to compose heterogeneous motion planners as an alternative to ad hoc rule-based heuristics, while preserving explainability.
IV-D
Computational Overhead
We compare the total simulation time of each configuration across the full Val14 benchmark on the same hardware.
Running
FlowDrive*
alone takes
53.8
min
53.8\text{\,}\mathrm{min}
, while
PDM-Closed
alone completes in
29.1
min
29.1\text{\,}\mathrm{min}
.
The full
Mosaic
framework, which evaluates both planners, scores, and verifies their trajectories, completes in
60.6
min
60.6\text{\,}\mathrm{min}
— an increase of only
13
%
13\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
over
FlowDrive*
alone.
Disabling the verification module saves less than
3
%
3\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
of computation time, indicating that the safety checks add negligible overhead.
Crucially,
FlowDrive*
runs on the GPU whereas
PDM-Closed
and all arbitration logic run entirely on the CPU, so the two planners occupy
different
compute resources.
In our benchmark they are evaluated sequentially, but because they are independent they can be executed in parallel, overlapping CPU and GPU work so that the added wall-clock latency of the second planner approaches zero.
Moreover, verification and emergency-stop logic were already part of each individual planner.
Mosaic
merely consolidates them into a shared module, adding no new computation.
The only true overhead is therefore the lightweight trajectory scoring, while the fallback mechanism operates lazily and only queries the
Emergency Stop
component if no verified trajectory is available.
IV-E
Ablation Studies
To isolate the contribution of each component, we conduct three ablation experiments on the CLS-R benchmark: (1) removing the verification module while keeping both planners (
w/o verif.
), (2) using only
FlowDrive*
with verification and emergency stop, and (3) using only
PDM-Closed
with verification and emergency stop.

IV-E
1
Component Contributions
Each AG component contributes measurably to the final result.
Removing the verification module more than doubles the number of at-fault collisions from 14 to 35, confirming that trajectory verification is essential for safety.
Using only a single planner with the AG framework further degrades both score and robustness.
Notably,
Mosaic
achieves fewer collisions (14) than either single-planner configuration (18 for
FlowDrive*
, 17 for
PDM-Closed
), demonstrating that the AG can leverage the safer trajectory across planners on a per-timestep basis.
For reference, both FlowDrive* and PDM-Closed produce 20 at-fault collisions each in their original implementations with their own internal verifiers.

Out of the 14 scenario types,
Mosaic

IV-E
3
Behavior Verification
As described in
Section
˜
III-B
, a trajectory is rejected when an at-fault collision is predicted.
If neither
FlowDrive*
nor
PDM-Closed
produces a verified trajectory, the
Emergency Stop
behavior is executed.
As shown in
Table
˜
III
,
PDM-Closed
fails verification in
0.30
%
0.30\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
and
FlowDrive*
in
0.29
%
0.29\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
of time steps.
At least one behavior fails verification in
0.50
%
0.50\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
of time steps, while both fail simultaneously in just
0.08
%
0.08\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
(141 out of
166 806
166\,806
time steps).
The low frequency of emergency stops illustrates the key benefit of the AG: in most cases where one planner proposes an unsafe trajectory, the other provides a valid alternative, preventing unnecessary interventions.
V
Conclusions
We presented
Mosaic
, a modular framework that uses arbitration graphs to fuse heterogeneous motion planners into an extensible, explainable decision-making structure with built-in verification and fallback mechanisms.
By combining the rule-based PDM-Closed and the learning-based
FlowDrive*
planner,
Mosaic
sets a new state of the art on the nuPlan Val14 closed-loop benchmarks with
95.48
95.48
CLS-NR and
93.98
93.98
CLS-R, while reducing at-fault collisions to just 14 — fewer than either planner in isolation.
On the interPlan benchmark,
Mosaic
scores
54.30
54.30
CLS-R, outperforming its best constituent planner by
23.3
%
23.3\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}
— all without retraining either planner or requiring additional training data.
Our ablation study shows that each framework component contributes to the result:
embedding verification within the AG structure, rather than inside each planner, makes every rejection decision traceable.
It allows the system to gracefully fall back to the alternative planner before engaging an emergency stop — reducing at-fault collisions while maintaining full transparency over the decision process.
The two planners exhibit complementary strengths across scenario types, and their combination outperforms either planner in isolation.
Because the framework treats planners as black-box behavior components, it generalizes to any planner and can evolve alongside future advances in both rule-based and learning-based motion planning.

preview_text:
Safe and explainable motion planning remains a central challenge in autonomous driving. While rule-based planners offer predictable and explainable behavior, they often fail to grasp the complexity and uncertainty of real-world traffic. Conversely, learned planners exhibit strong adaptability but suffer from reduced transparency and occasional safety violations. We introduce Mosaic, an extensible framework for structured decision-making that integrates both paradigms through arbitration graphs. By decoupling trajectory verification and scoring from the generation of trajectories by individual planners, every decision becomes transparent and traceable. Trajectory verification at a higher level introduces redundancy between the planners, limiting emergency braking to the rare case where all planners fail to produce a valid trajectory. Through unified scoring and optimal trajectory selection, rule-based and learned planners with complementary strengths and weaknesses can be combined to yield the best of both worlds. In experimental evaluation on nuPlan, Mosaic achieves 95.48 CLS-NR and 93.98 CLS-R on the Val14 closed-loop benchmark, setting a new state of the art, while reducing at-fault collisions by 30% compared to either planner in isolation. On the interPlan benchmark, focused on highly interactive and difficult scenarios, Mosaic scores 54.30 CLS-R, outperforming its best constituent planner by 23.3% - all without retraining or requiring additional data. The code is available at github.com/KIT-MRT/mosaic.

one_line_summary: 论文提出Mosaic框架，结合基于规则和学习的运动规划器用于自动驾驶，与强化学习、VLA等关键词无关。
